entries.title: COMMENTARY ON PAUL'S EPISTLES on romans 15:33
entries.excerpt: The God of peace is Christ, who said: "My peace I give to you, my peace I leave with you." This is what he prays for them, knowing that the Lord said: "Behold I am with you always, even to the end of the world." Paul therefore wants them to be the kind of people in whom the Lord Jesus Christ dwells, who has shown them that all the discord caused by human sin has been taken away and who has given them what is true, that they may live peacefully in that truth.

entries.title: Homily on Romans 30 on romans 15:33
entries.excerpt: "Now the God of peace be with you all. Amen."

entries.title: PELAGIUS'S COMMENTARY ON ROMANS on romans 15:33
entries.excerpt: The God of peace dwells only in those who are peaceable. It is good that he has concluded with peace, because the two parties are here called back to peaceful agreement with each other.

entries.title: INTERPRETATION OF THE LETTER TO THE ROMANS on romans 15:33
entries.excerpt: Paul called God the "God of peace" here for a reason, because he was concerned about those at Rome who were battling one another or at least who were suspicious of one another. He wanted them to be at peace with each other because of the controversy which they were having over the observance of the law.

entries.title: Commentary on Romans on romans 15:33
entries.excerpt: To join prayer to exhortation is the apostle's constant custom.

entries.title: Commentary on Romans on romans 15:33
entries.excerpt: Then when he says, now the God of peace, he shows that he is praying for them, saying: now the God, i.e., the giver of peace, be with you all, by the fact that you are at peace with one another: agree with one another and the God of peace will be with you (2 Cor 13:11). He adds, amen, i.e., let it be so: let the people say: amen (Ps 106:48).
